title: AI capex by company
question: Who is actually paying for the AI buildout, company by company - how big is each one's capex, how fast is it growing, and is each company funding the build out of its own cash or out of someone else's balance sheet?
finding: It is not a broad boom - it is five companies. Across 45 US-listed filers spanning the whole AI value chain, latest-fiscal-year capex totals about $520bn, but the top five (the hyperscalers - Amazon $132bn, Alphabet $91bn, Meta $70bn, Microsoft $65bn, Oracle $56bn) are 79% of it, Amazon alone a quarter, and their combined capex grew +73% in one year ($238bn -> $413bn). The rest of the chain barely spends: fabless silicon (Nvidia 2.8% capex/revenue, +$97bn FCF) and software (Palantir, Salesforce <2%) monetize the build rather than fund it. Following the cash settles the bigger question: in AGGREGATE the build pays for itself - the 45 names generated $910bn of operating cash against $520bn capex, a +$390bn surplus, and still returned $182bn to shareholders (against $176bn net new debt); even the five biggest spenders self-fund ($588bn hyperscaler OCF vs $413bn capex). So 'AI is a credit bubble' fails at the aggregate. The fragility is the funding, not the magnitude, and it is narrow: the debt is a CHOICE for the core, a NECESSITY for the edge. Alphabet added $37bn of net new debt - the same as Oracle - but Alphabet returned $46bn to holders and earned +$73bn FCF (cheap-debt-plus-buyback optimization), while Oracle returned nothing and burned -$24bn FCF (funding the gap). The tell that separates them is returning cash WHILE building: 32 of 45 self-fund (21 also return cash), just 7 are debt-funded (Oracle, 3 neoclouds, a REIT, 2 utilities) and only Intel is raising equity. The war chest agrees - Nvidia holds 7.5 years of capex in cash, the core ~1.3, Oracle 0.6, CoreWeave 0.3. Verdict conditional: the systemic risk is not the aggregate dollar figure; it is a small, nameable set (Oracle and the neoclouds) funding the build with outside money (debt for Oracle/CoreWeave, equity raises for the smaller neoclouds) while burning cash on the thinnest cushions - exactly the financing-refusal trigger of studies 27 and 30, now with names and numbers. Going global (16 foreign giants added, each from its own audited filing/earnings release, USD-converted and double-verified): the true total is ~$687bn across 61 companies ($520bn US + $167bn foreign), and concentration softens exactly as predicted - the top-5 US clouds fall from 79% of the US table to 60% of the global total - but they still lead (only TSMC $41bn and Samsung $37bn crack the top tier; the entire Chinese cloud sector, Alibaba+Tencent+Baidu+ByteDance ~$45bn, is out-spent by the US hyperscalers ~9:1). The one thing the US-only view badly missed: foundry & memory is the real #2 layer at $139bn and 78% offshore (TSMC/Samsung/SK Hynix/SMIC) - the chips are made where US filings can't see. Five-year funding history (FY2021-25, all 9 US+China hyperscalers, with tickers): the US clouds' cash reserves HELD or grew as capex went vertical (Alphabet/Amazon ~$125bn, MSFT ~$95bn) and NONE issued equity - they bought stock back; even the largest China cloud spends less than the smallest US hyperscaler. The financing instrument is a credit signal: investment-grade names (MSFT AAA ... every US+China hyperscaler) borrow cheap on their own name and return cash, while the junk/unrated edge (CoreWeave B+, the miners, Nebius/IREN unrated) must pledge GPUs for 7-10% secured debt or sell equity - so equity issuance in this build is the DISTRESS tell, not strength. Oracle is the canary (BBB/neg, buybacks cut to zero, cash drained $47bn->$10bn, +$37bn debt, one downgrade from junk). Debt schedule (all 20 names with capex>=$5bn): good credit borrowed cheap, FIXED and LONG - the IG core is ~100% fixed-rate, 14-18yr maturities, ~4% coupons, net cash, so a downturn leaves existing coupons untouched; the exposed are floating/short-dated - ByteDance ~95% floating, CoreWeave 51% floating at ~9% with 59% of its debt due within 3yr; two fuses = a rate move (floating names) and a downgrade (Oracle/Intel, BBB-neg, still issuing tens of billions of new debt). Also: 2026 guidance announced this year keeps accelerating (big four ~$710bn for 2026 vs ~$383bn actual) and 5 of 6 already raised it mid-year while supply-constrained, so further raises are the base case (cut is the tail); Apple (~$14bn) and Nvidia (~$9bn) barely move - the AI giants that don't build. Where the dollar GOES (bill of materials): top-heavy not pyramid-shaped - accelerator + memory + packaging ~85% of the AI-server BOM, while the 'foundation' layers (glass cloth, copper foil, photoresists) are tiny-dollar but absolute bottlenecks (Nittobo ~90% AI glass cloth, Ibiden ~70-80% GPU substrate, ASML 100% EUV). The great inversion: the capex reappears as the chipmakers' cash flow - chipmaker FCF exploded $16bn(2023)->$96bn(2025) while hyperscaler FCF rolled over, the gap collapsed +$197bn->+$102bn and crosses in 2026 (sellers' FCF overtakes spenders' for the first time; Bloomberg/GLJ). Source: SEC EDGAR XBRL for the 45 US filers + foreign 20-F anchors, gross PP&E capex + balance-sheet cash/debt + buybacks, latest full fiscal year; net new borrowing measured as the YoY change in the debt stock (not the CP-inflated issuance line); 16 foreign current-year figures from each company's own filing, verified against a second source. Caveat: capex is all PP&E, not AI-only; the foreign tier is well-sourced but less homogeneous (mixed currencies/FX, ByteDance private/estimated).
